message.content: Adversaries may exfiltrate data using unencrypted network protocols distinct from their command and control (C2) channels. This technique involves sending data to alternate network locations via protocols like HTTP, FTP, or DNS, often obfuscated but not encrypted. Common methods include embedding data within protocol headers or using encoding/compression algorithms such as base64. Notable examples include Agent Tesla using SMTP, FTP, and HTTP, APT32 encoding data in DNS subdomains, and FIN6 using HTTP POSTs. The impact of this technique is significant as it allows data theft without detection by traditional C2 monitoring. Mitigation strategies include network segmentation, monitoring for unusual data flows, implementing data loss prevention (DLP) solutions, and inspecting network traffic for anomalies. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce the risk of exploitation.
